properties.address: 2790 SHADY VALLEY DR NE, ATLANTA GA 30324
properties.tree_description: Remove two 22” DBH pine trees located at the left corner of the detached garage. Both trees exhibit obscured main stems, with the upper portion of each main stem displaying greater than 20% lean toward the structure. Additionally, the upper stem structure is notably curved, creating a reduced structural integrity and increased likelihood of failure under load.

The tree presents multiple structural concerns, including severe basal decay, weakened buttress roots, and extensive internal deterioration within the trunk and major limbs, resulting in reduced overall stability. Visible canopy decline, including thinning foliage and branch dieback, indicates diminished vitality and a limited ability to contain decay. Positioned over homes, utilities, and a dead-end roadway, the tree poses increased consequences in the event of failure, including blocked access and delayed emergency response. Ongoing environmental stressors further reduce the likelihood that mitigation measures will effectively lower risk.Reason for Removal:Dying tree

properties.tree_description:
Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits a pronounced circumferential constriction  mid/lower stem, consistent with long-term girdling from previously installed play equipment, resulting in radial growth restriction, visible cambial deformation, and localized tissue compression around the full circumference of the trunk. The condition has created a discontinuity within the lower bole with visible restriction of normal growth patterns, consistent with long-standing vascular disruption.
		
		
			Tree number:7Species:OakTree Size (DBH):11Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits multiple circumferential girdling points along the lower and mid-stem, consistent with prolonged restriction from previously installed play equipment, resulting in visible bark deformation and stem compression. The canopy is largely absent with minimal residual growth, consistent with advanced decline.
		
		
			Tree number:8Species:Oak Tree Size (DBH):11Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits circumferential girdling caused by long-standing play equipment straps embedded in the trunk, resulting in visible deformation and restricted stem development. Fungal fruiting bodies are present throughout the stem, indicating internal decay, with sparse remaining foliage.
		
		
			Tree number:9Species:River Birch Tree Size (DBH):25Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits evidence of prior topping or breakage events within the upper canopy, with multiple truncated leaders and irregular branching structure. Areas of decay are visible at former break points with localized wood degradation, and canopy structure appears uneven and consistent with past storm damage or mechanical failure.
		
		
			Tree number:15Species:Hackberry Tree Size (DBH):27Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits a large open cavity associated with a former co-dominant stem union, containing visible decay and loss of internal wood. Vegetative growth within the cavity suggests retained moisture conditions, with the defect located at a primary structural point.
		
		
			Tree number:16Species:Willow Tree Size (DBH):7Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits a failed co-dominant stem union in the upper canopy, with visible splitting and separation at the point of attachment, and one leader fractured leaving an open wound at the union.
		
		
			Tree number:17Species:White Mulberry Tree Size (DBH):16Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits a pronounced lean exceeding 20 degrees from vertical, Undesired species. 
		
		
			Tree number:18Species:Boxelder Tree Size (DBH):27Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits longitudinal cracking along the main stem with visible separation of wood fibers. A cavity is present along the trunk with associated internal decay and wood loss along the primary stem.
		
		
			Tree number:19Species:Maple Tree Size (DBH):7Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits areas of sloughing bark along the lower stem with associated frost cracking at the basal portion, with exposed tissue and early-stage degradation consistent with incipient decay.
		
		
			Tree number:24Species:Boxelder Tree Size (DBH):24Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits loss of a co-dominant leader with a visible tear-out at the former attachment point, with exposed wood and localized decay consistent with prior breakage and subsequent degradation within the upper stem and scaffold structure.
		
		
			Tree number:26Species:Black Cherry Tree Size (DBH):18Tree location:See site map Description of Tree:Numbered flagging tape Reason for Removal:OtherComments:Exhibits a documented lean of 27.3 degrees from vertical, measured in the field using a digital clinometer, with the main trunk oriented in the direction of the lean and canopy mass distributed accordingly.

properties.address: 869 HAVEN OAKS CT NE, ATLANTA GA 30342

properties.tree_description:
Pine tree exhibits significant soil erosion at base, resulting in exposure of the structural root system. Current lean is minimal but there is clear evidence of ongoing soil loss, which will continue to compromise integrity of root plate. Progressive erosion is expected to further destabilize tree, increasing likelihood of failure. Given the tree’s size and condition, this presents a foreseeable risk of structural failure.  Visible root exposure and soil displacement indicate partial loss of lateral support has already occurred. Tree is located in close proximity to two residences, placing structures and occupants within the potential fall zone. Next door neighbor reports tree sways dangerously during high winds. Neighbor reports existing fear of tree failure. Based on these conditions, the tree meets the criteria for hazardous or dying classification due to compromised structural stability and risk of failure.Reason for Removal:Dying treeComments:Pine tree exhibits significant soil erosion at base, resulting in exposure of the structural root system. Current lean is minimal but there is clear evidence of ongoing soil loss, which will continue to compromise integrity of root plate. Neighbor reports existing fear of tree failure. Based on these conditions, the tree meets the criteria for hazardous or dying classification due to compromised structural stability and risk of failure.
